text: Hi friends, this video demonstrates our technique of performing a laparoscopic appendectomy in a young male.

The surgeon utilizes the umbilical port as the first working port, the suprapubic port as the second working port, and the left iliac fossa port for the laparoscopic camera during the appendectomy procedure.

The surgical team demonstrates triangulation in a laparoscopic appendectomy, with the camera port positioned away from the area of interest to optimize visualization of the organ in a patient of small build.
The laparoscopic view reveals the presence of pus in the pouch of Douglas (POD), indicating infection or inflammation in the pelvic region.

The surgeon observes the peristalsis of the ureter and carefully positions the patient to ensure the small bowel is pushed away from the dissection field in the right iliac fossa, using head low and right lateral up positioning.

The surgeon uses the working port to identify the appendix, which is located below the cecum. Purulent fluid is visible in the right iliac fossa and is suctioned away. The surgeon pulls the tip of the appendix to better visualize its position.

The surgeon manipulates the acutely inflamed appendix, twisting it to reveal the attachment of the mesoappendix. The appendix is covered with inflammatory substances, and the surgeon prepares to use monopolar cautery for further dissection.

The surgeon uses a hook or Mediland instrument to dissect between the appendix and the mesoappendix, ensuring the appendix is isolated without bulky mesoappendix attached. This facilitates easy retrieval of the appendix through the 10mm camera or working port.
The surgeon identifies the location of the small bowel to ensure it is positioned away from the dissection area in the right iliac fossa.
And we have to always have an eye on the metal portion of the working hand so that there is no conducted energy passing to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon adjusts the traction on the appendix, gradually moving closer to the mesoappendix for precise dissection.
The surgeon applies traction to stretch the appendix away from the cecum, enhancing visibility of the attachment between the appendix and the mesoappendix.

The surgeon demonstrates effective triangulation to access the appendix and mesoappendix, twisting the appendix to clearly identify the mesoappendix. Short bursts of cautery are applied directly on top of the appendix for precise dissection.

The other major advantage of doing this kind of a dissection is that here we will have only very small branches of the appendicular artery coming to the appendix so that it's no big branch where there will be lot of bleeding or oozing that need not be really worried about at all from our end.
The surgeon adjusts the area of traction and counter-traction on the appendix to perform a precise dissection of the mesoappendix, ensuring careful manipulation of the tissue.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to dissect the mesoappendix from the appendix, with visible precision and control.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the metallic instrument to ensure it only contacts the targeted dissection area on the appendix, avoiding contact with surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the location of a small bubble and the base of the cecum, preparing for further dissection in the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects the mesoappendix, ensuring the dissection remains precise and does not extend onto the cecum.
The surgeon performs dissection close to the appendix, carefully manipulating the tissue to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully identifies the base of the appendix, ensuring the dissection remains close to the targeted area without extending too far.
The surgeon observes the elongated appendix, noting its extended appearance within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix, with the cecum visible beyond it.
The cecum is clearly visible in the surgical field, providing a reference point for the ongoing dissection near the appendix.
The surgeon identifies the base of the appendix by locating the convergence of the three tinea, a technique used to pinpoint the appendix in challenging cases, similar to open surgery.
So once again here you can see
The surgeon manipulates the instruments to achieve a precise angle for dissecting the bulky mesoappendix, preparing to separate it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon observes small areas of bleeding on the mesoappendix, visible as minor oozes during the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to work near important structures such as the small bowel and cecum, minimizing thermal spread for precise dissection.

The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery to apply short bursts of energy, effectively opening up tissue planes as traction pulls the appendix away from the cecum. The cecum provides counter traction from its fixed position in the retroiliac fossa.
The surgeon meticulously dissects through small, thin fibrous strands in the mesoappendix, carefully separating these structures with precision.
The surgeon adjusts the position of the assisting hand to change the traction on the appendix, pulling it away from surrounding tissues. The traction visibly peels off the adjacent tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies a small branch holding the mesoappendix in place, surrounded by adipose tissue that does not contribute to the structural strength.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the appendix, with visible separation of tissue planes facilitated by traction and careful manipulation.
The surgeon exposes the appendicular artery, revealing its course as it runs adjacent to the appendix, with surrounding tissue planes clearly visible.
The surgeon identifies the location of the small bowel and visually confirms the base of the appendix, preparing for further dissection.
The appendicular branch from the iliacolic artery is visible, supplying the appendix.

The surgeon uses a short burst of cautery with the Maryland dissector, adjusting its curvature to avoid the base of the cecum. The surgeon holds and pulls away strands from the appendix, applying cautery to sever them. A small vessel is visible running through the mesoappendix.

The surgeon coagulates a branch of the appendicular artery close to the appendix, ensuring the specimen is thin for easy extraction through the tenon port. The meticulous dissection prevents a bulky mesoappendix, facilitating smooth removal.
The surgeon uses cautery to perform dissection near the base of the appendix, gradually moving closer to the last branch of the appendicular artery.

The surgeon uses a bipolar cautery to coagulate a slightly bulky vessel near the base of the appendix, close to the cecum, to prevent bleeding from the retracted vessel. The vessel is held and coagulated with a safety margin.
The surgeon cuts the previously coagulated vessel, resulting in the retraction of the mesoappendix. The widening of the structure indicates the beginning of the cecum.
The surgeon holds the dissected tissue near the base of the appendix, revealing the cecum clearly in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs milking at the base of the appendix to push out any fecal material, ensuring a clear surgical field.

The surgeon prepares to use an endo loop with chromic catgut absorbable suture, carefully identifying the exact location of the appendix base. The loop is positioned slightly towards the appendix side, ensuring it does not encroach on the cecum.
The surgeon applies two endo loops in the proximal area and one loop distally on the appendix to prevent spillage from its lumen.
The surgeon places the second chromic catgut loop around the base of the appendix, ensuring it is positioned correctly to avoid encroaching on the cecum.

The surgeon places the second chromic catgut loop three to four millimeters away from the first loop at the base of the appendix, ensuring it is not too close or too far to prevent stump appendicitis.
The surgeon milks the lumen of the appendix to dislodge any ficolith into the distal portion, then applies the third chromic catgut loop away from the proximal loops to prevent spillage.

The surgeon performs a bubble walk to rule out Meckel's diverticulum, inspecting the bowel for any abnormalities. The appendicular specimen is then cut, revealing the presence of inflammatory fluid but no fecolith.
The surgeon has completed the cutting of the appendix, following the placement of loops to prevent spillage from the lumen.
Some people have a doubt whether this is a big stump.
The surgical instrument is shown opening to a width of 1.5 centimeters, demonstrating its range of motion during the procedure.
The surgeon observes the remaining stump of the appendix, measuring approximately four to five millimeters in length, after the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a bipolar cautery device to cauterize the mucosa of the appendicular stump, ensuring hemostasis and reducing the risk of infection.
If you're not very comfortable with this technique, it's always better to use a beaded and soaked gauze to have some chemical cauterization happening.
The surgeon changes the camera to a 5mm port and retrieves the appendix through a 10mm camera port, visually capturing the extraction process.
The surgeon pulls the appendix into the trocar, preparing for closure of the ten mm port using an absorbable suture with a suture passer.
The closed port is visible, indicating the completion of the procedure and secure closure of the surgical access point.
